# preprocess/

Synthetic conflict-pair corpus generator (IMPLEMENTATION.md M2,
experimental_plans.tex S1.2-S1.7). Emits packed-token shards under
`data/processed/<dataset_name>/` in the on-disk format
`training/data.py:PackedTokenDataset` expects (`meta.json` + `*.bin` shards), plus a
generation-parameters JSON recorded under `results/`.

Content (names, institutions, vignette text, etc.) is never hardcoded in this module
-- it is generated by calling an LLM (gpt-4o-mini by default, switched from GPT-4.1 on
cost grounds), and the scripts below only orchestrate those calls, parse/validate the
output, and (separately) verify it against the production GPT-2 tokenizer. Training
text is now LLM-authored whole-paragraph vignettes, not mechanically assembled from
per-relation-type templates (S1 revision (g)); the original templates are kept and
regenerated, repurposed as a fixed eval-only probing format (S1.7), not used for
training text. Nothing under `data_pools/` is checked into git (see `.gitignore`); it
is regenerated by the scripts below.

## Pipeline order

1. `schema.py` -- registry of every pool (name pools, value pools) and the 14
   relation-type inventory (S1.2). Pure data, no content.
2. `prompts.py` -- LLM prompt text for pool generation and paraphrase-template
   generation. Pure strings, no content.
3. `generation/client.py` -- OpenAI (gpt-4o-mini by default) call wrapper shared by
   the generation scripts: preflight checks (fails fast if `OPENAI_API_KEY`/the `openai` package are
   missing, before any network call), retry with exponential backoff (5 attempts), and
   batch sizing with >=20% leeway over the strict remaining count (absorbs rejected
   duplicates without a guaranteed extra round-trip), capped at 150 entries/call so a
   single completion can't be asked for an unreasonably long response.
   `generation/dedup.py` -- post-generation cleaning: drops empty/malformed entries,
   LLM refusal artifacts, and (for name pools) anything not shaped like "Firstname
   Lastname"; dedups case/whitespace-insensitively. Runs inline per batch and standalone
   (`python -m preprocess.generation.dedup --pool entity_names`) to re-clean a saved pool.
4. `generate_pools.py` -- calls the LLM to fill each pool in `schema.py` (15 pools: the
   entity-name and mentor-name pools, and 13 relation-type value pools -- no anchor
   pool, dropped per S1 revision), iterating in batches until the target size is hit.
   Run manually per pool, e.g.:
   ```
   python -m preprocess.generate_pools --pool entity_names
   python -m preprocess.generate_pools --pool mentor_names
   python -m preprocess.generate_pools --pool alma_mater_values
   ... (one per PoolSpec in schema.py; --dry-run prints the prompt without calling the API)
   ```
   Output: `data_pools/<pool_name>.json` (a flat JSON array of strings).
5. `generate_templates.py` -- calls the LLM to write a first-mention template + >=5
   paraphrase restatement templates per relation type. **Not used for training text
   anymore** (superseded by vignette generation, step 9b below, S1 revision (g)) --
   kept and regenerated purely as a fixed, controlled **eval-only probing format**
   (S1.7): since side A/B training vignettes share no common stem, per-fact recall is
   scored on these templates instead, where the two sides still diverge at exactly
   one token by construction (S1.3). Run manually, e.g.
   `python -m preprocess.generate_templates --all`.
   Output: `data_pools/templates/<relation_key>.json`.
6. `divergence.py` -- tokenizer-based surface-form divergence check (S1.3). Pure code,
   no LLM calls, runs anywhere (GPT-2 tokenizer only, no model weights). `check_pair()`
   verifies, on demand, that two *specific* values (a would-be contested pair) diverge
   at the same token position when rendered through a given template -- catches cases
   like "New York"/"New Jersey" sharing a first token that would break the
   single-token recall-scoring assumption. Not a pool-wide pre-check: only the pair an
   entity actually gets assigned as its contested values is checked (`entities.py`),
   not every pair in the pool. `python -m preprocess.divergence` runs a self-contained
   smoke test.
7. `validate_pools.py` -- cross-pool disjointness (name pools globally unique across
   each other; every value pool checked against every other, per S1.2) + an optional
   WikiText-103 proper-noun overlap screen (regex heuristic, no NER/LM model loaded).
   Run after step 4: `python -m preprocess.validate_pools [--wikitext-path ...]`.
8. `scheduler.py` -- split levels, balanced relation-type assignment, and exposure-event
   placement (S1.2, S1.3, S1.6). No recency, no designed grid: `SPLIT_LEVELS` (the
   swept (n_A, n_B) split of a fixed budget T) and `build_assignment_list()` (one
   shuffled (contested relation_key, split level) replicate list -- every relation type
   used as the contested type the same number of times, at every split level, without
   a per-cell configuration table) and `background_relation_keys()` (fixed cyclic
   offset from the contested type, so background-type usage balances for free once
   contested-type usage does). Also `schedule_entity_occurrences()`: places all
   n_A + n_B occurrence events for an entity i.i.d. uniformly at random across the full
   planned token stream, then labels each A/B by thread. Pure code, no content.
9. `entities.py` -- builds the population (S1.6: 14 relation types x split levels x 20
   replicates, proposed default 1,680 entities) by walking `scheduler.py`'s balanced
   assignment list. Every entity has exactly 5 facts: 1 contested relation (two
   candidate values + a split) and K=4 background relations (single value each, from
   the fixed cyclic offset) -- no anchor. Values are drawn via `PoolCycler`
   (round-robin over a shuffled pool, near-exact usage balance, rather than i.i.d.
   resampling); the contested pair is checked for surface-form divergence on-demand
   (`divergence.check_pair`) before being accepted. Pure code, consumes pool/template
   JSON.
   `render_sample.py`/`render_utils.py` -- visual QA: draws a small ad-hoc sample of
   vignettes (not the full population) and renders one occurrence instance per side,
   reusing `entities.py`'s divergence-checked pair drawing and `scheduler.py`'s
   background-offset rule so the sample matches what the real corpus would produce.
   `python -m preprocess.render_sample --n 5`.
10. `prompts.py:vignette_prompt` -- LLM-authored biography generation (S1.2/S1.4, S1
    revision (g)): given a name and 5 flat facts (no hint of contestedness), asks for
    V independent whole-paragraph variants, each required to state every value
    verbatim (case-insensitive, S1.7) -- checked later since a common-noun value
    reliably gets case-folded mid-sentence by a fluent writer.
    `generate_vignette_sample.py` -- ad-hoc single-example QA tool (one hand-specified
    entity, not the population), `python -m preprocess.generate_vignette_sample`.
    `generate_vignettes.py` -- population-scale batch generator: one call per
    (entity, side) = 3,360 calls, issued concurrently (async, semaphore-bounded,
    default 10 in-flight), with per-call value-fidelity retry and resumability (an
    (entity_id, side) already written to disk with enough variants is skipped, so a
    killed/interrupted run picks up where it left off).
    `python -m preprocess.generate_vignettes --concurrency 15`.
    Output: `data_pools/vignettes/<entity_id>_<side>.json`.
11. `backbone.py` -- downloads WikiText-103 (train/val/test) via HuggingFace `datasets`
    and drops exact-duplicate lines. Pure code, no LLM calls, needs network access.
    `python -m preprocess.backbone --out-dir data/raw/wikitext-103`. Note: uses the
    `Salesforce/wikitext` HF repo id -- the bare `wikitext` legacy loading-script name
    is deprecated and fails on current `datasets` versions.
12. `assemble_corpus.py` -- interleaves the backbone with entities' vignette
    occurrences (S1.7) into packed shards. Injections snap to backbone line
    boundaries (never mid-line) and are EOS-delimited on both sides, same as backbone
    lines, so each is its own self-contained document. `--total-tokens` is a
    *backbone* budget, not the final corpus length -- injected content adds its own
    tokens on top, and there's a fixed injected-token floor (population size x T,
    independent of `--total-tokens`) that the budget needs to be large relative to
    for injected content to actually read as a small fraction (S1.7 has the measured
    numbers). Also writes `results/occurrence_log.json` (entity/side/final token
    position per occurrence, for exposure-count reconstruction and for the training
    loop to derive dense-checkpoint windows).
    `python -m preprocess.assemble_corpus --total-tokens 100000000 --out-dir data/processed/<name>`.

## Not yet implemented

- Vignette generation is incomplete (see experimental_plans.tex's STATUS note for
  exact counts); resume with `python -m preprocess.generate_vignettes` (resumable).
- The eval-time probing harness that actually reads `data_pools/templates/` +
  `results/population.json` and scores per-fact recall at a checkpoint doesn't exist
  yet -- only the templates themselves (repurposed for this, S1.7) are in place.
- `total_tokens`/`T`/`V` are still pending the exposure-budget pilot (S1.11); the
  corpus assembler has only been smoke-tested at dev scale, not run for the real
  corpus.

## Local development notes

- A project-local virtualenv (`.venv/`, gitignored) has `transformers` installed for
  tokenizer-only use (no torch) -- sufficient for `divergence.py`, `entities.py`, and
  `assemble_corpus.py` without a GPU. `datasets` is needed for `backbone.py` (also
  used by `assemble_corpus.py` to load `Salesforce/wikitext`) and `openai` for the
  `generate_*.py` scripts (the latter also needs `OPENAI_API_KEY` set).
- Per CLAUDE.md S5: nothing in this module loads or runs a language model, only its
  tokenizer, so all of the above is safe to run on a machine with no GPU.
